title: Why 12 Months Is the Most Important Number in Your Commercial Lease
excerpt: Most Newcastle business owners start thinking about their lease when they get the renewal notice. By then, the leverage is gone. Here is what to do — and when.
metaTitle: 12-Month Commercial Lease Strategy for Newcastle Business Owners | Your Office Space
metaDescription: The most powerful thing a commercial tenant can do is start 12 months out. Here is why — and exactly what to do before your renewal notice arrives.
author: Joe Kelley
body:
There is a moment every Newcastle business owner hits at some point.

The renewal notice arrives. The landlord's agent is on the phone. You have 30 days to decide whether you are staying, going, or signing whatever is in front of you.

Most people sign. Not because the deal is good, but because moving is hard, time is short, and the alternative feels overwhelming.

That is the moment the landlord's agent has been working toward for months. And it almost always costs the tenant more than it should.

## The 12-month rule

In commercial leasing, 12 months to lease expiry is the inflection point. Before that, you have options. After it, you are increasingly cornered.

Here is what 12 months gives you.

**Time to understand what you actually have.** Most tenants have never properly read their lease. They do not know what the make-good clause requires, how rent reviews are calculated, or what their option terms actually say. Twelve months gives you time to find out before those things become urgent.

**Genuine market comparison.** What is available in Newcastle right now? What incentives are landlords offering new tenants? What comparable spaces exist in your precinct? With time, you can get real answers. Under pressure, you cannot.

**Negotiating position.** The most powerful thing a tenant can do is demonstrate they have a credible alternative. A competing brief — even one you are not serious about — gives you leverage. That leverage disappears when your expiry is 60 days out.

**The ability to push back on rent reviews.** Most commercial leases include fixed rent reviews of 3–4% per year, or market reviews tied to CPI or comparable rents. With 12 months, you can challenge a review, get independent market evidence, and negotiate from a position of knowledge rather than panic.

**Make-good planning.** Make-good obligations — the requirement to restore the premises to original condition at lease end — can cost anywhere from $10,000 to $200,000 depending on what you have done to the space. Twelve months gives you time to scope it properly, negotiate a cap, or arrange a make-good waiver in exchange for a renewal at a good rent.

## What happens when you wait

We see it constantly. A business in Newcastle signs a lease renewal in a hurry. They accept the rent increase because they could not verify whether it was fair. They do not push back on the make-good clause because there is no time to get advice. They miss the window to argue a market rent review because the deadline passed.

Three years later, they are sitting in the same space, paying more than they should, and wondering how it got here.

One client came to us with eight weeks to expiry. Their landlord was demanding a 22% rent increase. We pushed back hard, ran a competing brief on a nearby property, and got the increase down to 6% with a three-month rent-free period. But eight weeks is tight. Twelve months, we could have done better.

## What to do right now

If you have 12 months to run on your current lease — or you are not sure when it expires — start now.

Not with a solicitor. Not with a panic call to the landlord. Start by understanding your current position.

**Step 1: Find your expiry date.** Pull out your lease and find the commencement date, term, and any options. If you cannot find it, contact your landlord or building manager and ask for a copy. You have every right to it.

**Step 2: Check your make-good obligations.** Read the make-good clause carefully. Does it require full reinstatement? Or a fair wear and tear standard? The difference can be tens of thousands of dollars.

**Step 3: Understand your rent review mechanism.** Is your next review fixed (e.g. 3.5% per year) or market-based? If market-based, what does a market rent review process look like under your lease? Many tenants do not know they have the right to dispute a market review with independent evidence.

**Step 4: Get an independent view.** Before you talk to the landlord, get a view on what comparable rents look like in your area. This does not have to cost anything — call us, and we will tell you what we are seeing in the Newcastle market.

**Step 5: Start your option process early.** If you have an option to renew, most leases require you to exercise it within a specific window — often 6–9 months before expiry. Miss that window and the option lapses. You are then negotiating a new lease from scratch with no leverage.
